rationale: Gel column: microtubes containing gel (dextran acrylamide) with or without anti-sera. Centrifugation: agglutinated cells trapped at top/within gel = positive. Non-agglutinated cells pass through to bottom = negative. Standardized, objective, stable results.

stem: An organism isolated from a dog bite wound is a gram-negative rod that is oxidase-positive, catalase-positive, and indole-positive. It grows on blood agar but not MacConkey. The organism is:
rationale: Pasteurella multocida: gram-negative coccobacillus from dog/cat bites. Oxidase+, catalase+, indole+, grows on blood/chocolate but NOT MacConkey. Rapid onset cellulitis at bite site. Penicillin is treatment of choice.

stem: An anti-Jo-1 antibody is most specifically associated with:
rationale: Anti-Jo-1 (anti-histidyl-tRNA synthetase): most common antisynthetase antibody. Associated with polymyositis/dermatomyositis with interstitial lung disease, mechanic's hands, Raynaud phenomenon, and arthritis. Part of antisynthetase syndrome.

stem: A positive result for fetal fibronectin (fFN) in vaginal secretions at 24 weeks gestation indicates:
options: Increased risk of preterm delivery within 7-14 days
rationale: Fetal fibronectin: glycoprotein at maternal-fetal interface. Normally absent from vaginal secretions between 22-35 weeks. Positive fFN: increased risk of preterm delivery. Negative fFN: >99% NPV that delivery will NOT occur within 7-14 days.

stem: Laboratory safety showers must be tested:
options: Weekly activation to verify function (per ANSI Z358.1)
rationale: ANSI Z358.1: safety showers activated weekly to verify operation and flush standing water. Eyewash stations: also weekly activation. Full inspection and maintenance annually. Must be within 10 seconds (55 feet) of hazard area. Tepid water (60-100F).

stem: A patient's anti-Xa level is used to monitor:
options: Low molecular weight heparin (enoxaparin) and unfractionated heparin in some situations
rationale: Anti-Xa assay monitors LMWH (enoxaparin, dalteparin) and can monitor unfractionated heparin (alternative to aPTT in heparin resistance, lupus anticoagulant). LMWH has more predictable pharmacokinetics; anti-Xa used for obese patients, renal impairment, pregnancy.

stem: Specimen rejection criteria include all of the following EXCEPT:
options: Specimen collected in wrong tube type
Specimen with two unique identifiers matching the requisition
rationale: A properly labeled specimen with matching identifiers is acceptable. Rejection criteria: unlabeled/mislabeled, wrong tube, hemolyzed (for affected analytes), clotted (for CBC), insufficient volume, expired specimen, improper transport conditions.

stem: Non-hemolytic febrile reactions during transfusion can be prevented by:
rationale: FNHTR prevention: prestorage leukoreduction removes WBCs before cytokine accumulation during storage. More effective than bedside filtration. Reduces >99.9% of WBCs (<5 x 10^6 residual).

stem: An organism from a chronic wound shows branching, filamentous gram-positive rods with sulfur granules in tissue. The organism is:
rationale: Branching gram-positive rods + sulfur granules (yellow, crushed show 'ray fungus' appearance) = Actinomyces israelii. Anaerobic/microaerophilic. Causes cervicofacial, thoracic, and abdominal actinomycosis. NOT acid-fast (distinguishes from Nocardia). Treatment: prolonged penicillin.

stem: Total bilirubin 15.2 mg/dL with direct bilirubin 12.8 mg/dL. Urine bilirubin is positive, urobilinogen is decreased. ALP is 450 U/L. This pattern indicates:
rationale: Predominantly direct (conjugated) hyperbilirubinemia + bilirubinuria + decreased urobilinogen + elevated ALP = obstructive jaundice (bile duct obstruction). Conjugated bilirubin is water-soluble -> appears in urine. Decreased urobilinogen: bile not reaching intestine.

stem: Immunochromatographic (lateral flow) assays used in rapid testing work by:
options: Capillary action moving sample across a membrane with immobilized antibodies creating visible lines
rationale: Lateral flow immunoassay: sample migrates via capillary action across nitrocellulose membrane. Target antigen binds labeled antibody -> complex captured at test line -> visible colored line = positive. Control line validates test. Used for rapid strep, pregnancy, COVID-19, drugs of abuse.

stem: Urine microscopy shows envelope-shaped (octahedral) crystals. These are:
rationale: Calcium oxalate dihydrate: octahedral/envelope-shaped crystals. Found in acidic to neutral urine. Common finding (dietary oxalate from spinach, chocolate, tea, rhubarb). Also seen in ethylene glycol poisoning (monohydrate dumbbell shapes are more concerning).

stem: Root cause analysis (RCA) in the laboratory is performed:
options: After a serious adverse event or sentinel event to identify system failures and prevent recurrence
rationale: RCA: systematic investigation after adverse/sentinel events (wrong blood transfused, misidentified specimen, critical value not reported). Identifies root causes (not just proximate causes), develops corrective/preventive actions (CAPA). Focuses on system failures, not blame.

stem: The JAK2 V617F mutation is found in >95% of patients with:
rationale: JAK2 V617F: gain-of-function mutation in >95% of polycythemia vera, ~50% of essential thrombocythemia, and ~50% of primary myelofibrosis. BCR-ABL negative myeloproliferative neoplasms. WHO diagnostic criterion for PV.

stem: A pregnancy test (urine hCG) may be falsely negative if:
options: The test is performed too early (before implantation, hCG levels undetectable)
The test is performed at midday
rationale: False negative hCG: testing too early (before hCG reaches detectable threshold, typically 25-50 mIU/mL for urine tests). hCG detectable ~10-14 days post-conception. Very dilute urine may also cause false negatives. Hook effect at very high hCG (rare, molar pregnancy).

stem: Gel electrophoresis of PCR products shows a band in the no-template control (NTC). This indicates:
options: Contamination of reagents or workspace with target DNA
rationale: NTC (negative control with no template DNA) should show NO band. A band in NTC = contamination (amplicon carryover, environmental DNA). Must investigate source, decontaminate, and repeat. Use separate pre/post-PCR areas and filtered tips.

stem: The proper response when a chemical splash occurs on skin is:
options: Remove contaminated clothing and flush affected area with water for 15-20 minutes
rationale: Chemical skin splash: remove contaminated clothing, flush with copious water for 15-20 minutes minimum. Do NOT attempt neutralization on skin (exothermic reaction risk). Seek medical evaluation. Document incident. Check SDS for specific first aid measures.

stem: Phenotypically, an Rh-null individual lacks all Rh antigens. Their RBCs characteristically show:
options: Stomatocytes and mild compensated hemolytic anemia
rationale: Rh-null: absence of all Rh antigens (extremely rare). RBCs show stomatocytic morphology, increased osmotic fragility, and mild compensated hemolytic anemia (Rh-null syndrome). Rh proteins are integral membrane proteins important for RBC membrane integrity.

stem: Serum tryptase level drawn 1-4 hours after an event is elevated. This supports a diagnosis of:
options: Anaphylaxis or systemic mast cell activation
rationale: Tryptase: released from mast cell degranulation. Peaks 1-2 hours after anaphylaxis, returns to baseline by 6-8 hours. Elevated tryptase supports anaphylaxis diagnosis, especially when clinical picture is unclear (perioperative events, post-mortem). Also elevated in mastocytosis (persistent elevation).

stem: An aPTT mixing study that corrects immediately indicates:
options: Factor deficiency (patient serum lacks a clotting factor)
rationale: Mixing study: patient plasma mixed 1:1 with normal pooled plasma. Correction of prolonged aPTT = factor deficiency (normal plasma provides the missing factor). No correction = inhibitor (LA, factor inhibitor). Incubation at 37C for 2 hours distinguishes LA from time-dependent factor VIII inhibitor.

stem: Serum prealbumin (transthyretin) is used as a marker for:
options: Nutritional status and acute changes in protein synthesis (short half-life ~2 days)
rationale: Prealbumin (transthyretin): short half-life (~2 days) makes it sensitive marker for acute nutritional changes. Used to monitor nutritional status and response to nutritional support. Albumin half-life ~20 days (too slow for acute monitoring). Prealbumin is a negative acute-phase reactant (decreases in inflammation).

stem: Modified acid-fast stain (using 1% sulfuric acid instead of standard 3% HCl) is used to identify:
rationale: Modified (weak) acid-fast: 1% sulfuric acid or 0.5-1% HCl as decolorizer. Nocardia: partially acid-fast. Cryptosporidium oocysts: modified acid-fast positive (modified Kinyoun). Standard acid-fast (3% HCl): mycobacteria only.

stem: A post-transfusion blood sample shows positive DAT. The eluate does not react with any panel cells. Pre-transfusion DAT was negative. The most likely explanation is:
options: Passively transferred antibody from donor plasma coating recipient's RBCs
rationale: Positive DAT post-transfusion with non-reactive eluate and negative pre-transfusion DAT: passively acquired antibody from donor plasma (anti-A or anti-B in O donor plasma coating recipient A/B RBCs). If eluate showed specificity, would suggest an alloantibody or autoantibody.

stem: Serum free hemoglobin is elevated, haptoglobin is undetectable, and urine hemosiderin (Prussian blue of urine sediment) is positive. This indicates:
rationale: Free hemoglobin + undetectable haptoglobin + hemosiderinuria = chronic intravascular hemolysis. Hemosiderin in urine appears days after onset of intravascular hemolysis (iron from hemoglobin taken up by renal tubular cells, shed into urine). Seen in PNH, mechanical heart valves, march hemoglobinuria.

stem: Gene therapy and cell therapy products require molecular testing to verify:
options: Transgene integration, vector copy number, and absence of replication-competent vector
rationale: Gene/cell therapy molecular monitoring: verify transgene expression and persistence, vector copy number (qPCR), absence of replication-competent lentivirus/retrovirus (RCL/RCR testing), insertional mutagenesis screening. Emerging area in clinical molecular diagnostics.

stem: A laboratory safety officer determines that employees are not completing annual bloodborne pathogen training. The regulatory consequence is:
options: OSHA citation and potential fines -- BBP training is mandatory annually for all at-risk employees
rationale: OSHA mandates annual BBP training for all employees with occupational exposure to blood/OPIM. Failure to comply: OSHA citations, fines (up to $15,625 per serious violation, $156,259 for willful/repeat). Training must be during working hours at no cost to employees. Must include ECP review, modes of transmission, PPE use, post-exposure procedures.

stem: A CBC reveals WBC 15.2 x10^9/L, RBC 4.1 x10^12/L, Hgb 125 g/L, Hct 0.37, MCV 90 fL, MCH 30.5 pg, MCHC 338 g/L, PLT 450 x10^9/L. The differential shows 78% neutrophils, 4% bands, 12% lymphocytes, 4% monocytes, 2% eosinophils. This pattern is most consistent with:
options: Acute bacterial infection with left shift
rationale: Elevated WBC with neutrophilia (78% segs + 4% bands = 82% granulocytes) and left shift (bands >3%) indicates acute bacterial infection. Reactive thrombocytosis (PLT 450) is also consistent with acute infection/inflammation. Normal RBC indices rule out anemia.

stem: A patient's arterial blood gas shows pH 7.28, pCO2 55 mmHg, HCO3- 25 mEq/L, pO2 58 mmHg. The acid-base status is:
rationale: pH <7.35 = acidosis. Elevated pCO2 (55 mmHg, normal 35-45) indicates respiratory cause. HCO3- is normal (22-26 mEq/L), indicating no metabolic compensation yet — uncompensated respiratory acidosis. Low pO2 suggests hypoventilation or V/Q mismatch.

stem: A urine culture yields >100,000 CFU/mL of a Gram-negative rod that is lactose-positive on MacConkey agar, indole-positive, and produces a metallic green sheen on EMB agar. The organism is:
rationale: E. coli: Gram-negative rod, lactose fermenter (pink colonies on MacConkey), indole-positive (tryptophan → indole via tryptophanase), metallic green sheen on EMB (strong acid production from lactose). Most common cause of uncomplicated UTI. Klebsiella is lactose+ but indole-negative. Proteus is lactose-negative.

stem: During antibody identification, a patient's serum reacts with all panel cells at the antiglobulin phase. Autocontrol is positive. The next step is:
options: Report as incompatible and refuse transfusion
Perform an autoabsorption or allogeneic adsorption to remove autoantibody and identify underlying alloantibodies
rationale: Panreactive serum + positive autocontrol = warm autoantibody coating patient's own RBCs. Autoabsorption (if not recently transfused) or allogeneic adsorption (if recently transfused) removes the autoantibody, allowing detection of clinically significant underlying alloantibodies. Must rule out alloantibodies before transfusion.

stem: A serology test for anti-nuclear antibodies (ANA) shows a homogeneous pattern at 1:320 titer on HEp-2 cells. This pattern is most associated with antibodies to:
rationale: Homogeneous ANA pattern: antibodies directed against dsDNA, histones, or nucleosomes. Strong association with SLE (anti-dsDNA) and drug-induced lupus (anti-histone). Centromere pattern = limited scleroderma (CREST). Speckled = extractable nuclear antigens. Nucleolar = scleroderma/Scl-70.

stem: A CSF analysis shows WBC 350/μL with 90% neutrophils, glucose 20 mg/dL (serum glucose 100 mg/dL), protein 250 mg/dL, and a positive Gram stain showing Gram-positive diplococci. The most likely diagnosis is:
options: Bacterial meningitis due to Streptococcus pneumoniae
rationale: CSF findings classic for bacterial meningitis: elevated WBC with neutrophilic predominance, very low glucose (<40% of serum = <40 mg/dL), elevated protein (>100 mg/dL). Gram-positive diplococci = S. pneumoniae, the most common cause of bacterial meningitis in adults. Viral meningitis shows lymphocytic predominance with normal glucose.

stem: A Levey-Jennings chart shows the last 7 QC values on the same side of the mean, though all are within 2 SD. According to Westgard rules, this represents:
options: An acceptable QC run with no action needed
A 7T violation indicating a systematic trend requiring investigation
A random error requiring immediate recalibration
rationale: The 7T (trend) rule: 7 consecutive QC values trending in one direction (increasing or decreasing). This differs from the 7x rule (7 values on one side of mean). Both indicate systematic error — shift (7x) or drift (7T). Even within 2SD, the pattern is non-random and requires investigation of reagent deterioration, calibration drift, or environmental changes.

stem: A laboratory is implementing a new chemistry analyzer. The validation protocol requires at minimum:
options: Running 5 patient samples and comparing to the old analyzer
Precision, accuracy, linearity, reportable range verification, reference range verification, and method comparison studies
rationale: New method validation per CLIA/CAP requires: precision (within-run and between-run reproducibility), accuracy (comparison to reference method or known standards), linearity (analytical measurement range), reportable range verification, reference range verification or transference, and method comparison with existing methodology. CLSI EP documents guide validation protocols.

stem: A real-time PCR assay for MRSA screening shows a cycle threshold (Ct) value of 38 in a sample, while the positive control has a Ct of 22. The high Ct value in the patient sample indicates:
options: Very low target concentration near the limit of detection
rationale: Ct value is inversely proportional to initial target quantity. High Ct (>35) = very low starting template, possibly near the assay's limit of detection. Results near LOD should be interpreted cautiously — may represent colonization vs contamination. Low Ct = high target concentration. The positive control at Ct 22 confirms assay functionality.

stem: A laboratory technologist notices that the formaldehyde monitor in the histology area reads 0.85 ppm (TWA). The OSHA permissible exposure limit for formaldehyde is 0.75 ppm TWA. The appropriate action is:
options: Continue working since it is close to the limit
Immediately evacuate and close the area, notify safety officer, implement engineering controls to reduce exposure below PEL
Switch to a different fixative for the day
rationale: OSHA PEL for formaldehyde: 0.75 ppm TWA, STEL 2 ppm. At 0.85 ppm, exposure exceeds the PEL — must stop work, notify safety officer, investigate cause (failed ventilation, spill), implement corrective action (repair/upgrade fume hood, improve ventilation). Medical surveillance required for employees exposed above action level (0.5 ppm). Formaldehyde is a known carcinogen (IARC Group 1).

stem: A peripheral blood smear from a patient with pancytopenia shows oval macrocytes, hypersegmented neutrophils (6+ lobes), and Howell-Jolly bodies. The MCV is 118 fL. The most likely cause is:
options: Megaloblastic anemia due to vitamin B12 or folate deficiency
rationale: Classic megaloblastic triad: oval macrocytes (MCV >100 fL), hypersegmented neutrophils (≥5 lobes in ≥5% or any with ≥6 lobes), and pancytopenia. B12/folate deficiency impairs DNA synthesis → megaloblastic changes in all cell lines. Howell-Jolly bodies (nuclear remnants) indicate impaired nuclear maturation. Confirm with B12, folate, methylmalonic acid, and homocysteine levels.

stem: A patient's serum potassium is reported as 7.2 mEq/L. Before calling the critical value, the technologist should first:
options: Report the value immediately without verification
Check the specimen for hemolysis and verify the result by repeat testing
Order an ECG for the patient
rationale: Critically elevated potassium (>6.0 mEq/L) can be artifactual from hemolysis (K+ released from lysed RBCs), difficult venipuncture (tourniquet time, fist clenching), or clotting in the tube. Must inspect specimen for hemolysis, check hemolysis index, and repeat if suspect. If confirmed, report immediately as critical value — hyperkalemia can cause fatal cardiac arrhythmias.

stem: A wound culture from a diabetic foot ulcer grows a Gram-positive coccus that is catalase-positive, coagulase-positive (both slide and tube), and produces beta-hemolysis on blood agar. DNase test is positive. The organism is:
rationale: S. aureus identification: catalase-positive (Staphylococcus, not Streptococcus), coagulase-positive (distinguishes from CoNS like S. epidermidis), beta-hemolytic, DNase-positive. Leading cause of skin/soft tissue infections. Diabetic foot ulcers are polymicrobial but S. aureus is a primary pathogen. Must test for MRSA (cefoxitin disk or mecA/PBP2a).

stem: A prenatal patient is typed as O-negative with a negative antibody screen at 28 weeks gestation. The standard immunoprophylaxis protocol requires:
options: No treatment needed since antibody screen is negative
Administer Rh Immune Globulin (RhIG) at 28 weeks antepartum and within 72 hours postpartum if the newborn is Rh-positive
Type and crossmatch 2 units of blood for standby
Repeat antibody screen monthly until delivery
rationale: RhIG (Rh Immune Globulin) prevents Rh alloimmunization in D-negative mothers carrying D-positive fetuses. Standard protocol: 300 μg RhIG at 28 weeks antepartum + within 72 hours postpartum (if newborn is D-positive). Additional doses for sensitizing events (amniocentesis, trauma, miscarriage). Rosette test/KB stain for fetomaternal hemorrhage >30 mL to determine additional doses needed.

stem: A patient's serum shows elevated CRP (85 mg/L), elevated ESR (65 mm/hr), and positive rheumatoid factor (RF) at 1:256. RF testing by nephelometry detects antibodies directed against:
rationale: Rheumatoid factor (RF) is an autoantibody (usually IgM) directed against the Fc portion of IgG. Elevated in RA (60-80% sensitivity) but not specific — also positive in SLE, Sjögren's, hepatitis C, endocarditis, and elderly patients. Anti-CCP (anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide) has higher specificity for RA. CRP and ESR are non-specific inflammatory markers.

stem: A synovial fluid from a swollen knee shows WBC 48,000/μL (95% neutrophils), low viscosity, poor mucin clot, glucose 25 mg/dL, and negatively birefringent needle-shaped crystals under polarized microscopy. The diagnosis is:
rationale: Gout: monosodium urate (MSU) crystals are needle-shaped and negatively birefringent (yellow when parallel to compensator axis). Synovial fluid is inflammatory (WBC >2,000, >75% neutrophils). Pseudogout: calcium pyrophosphate (CPP) crystals are rhomboid-shaped and weakly positively birefringent. Despite the high WBC, crystal identification confirms gout — but must also culture to rule out concurrent septic arthritis.

stem: A laboratory's QC data shows a coefficient of variation (CV) of 8% for a glucose assay. The manufacturer's stated CV is 3%. The most likely cause and action is:
options: Imprecision exceeding manufacturer specifications — investigate for pipetting errors, reagent deterioration, temperature fluctuation, or instrument malfunction
rationale: CV = (SD/mean) × 100. Lab CV of 8% greatly exceeds manufacturer's 3% specification, indicating poor precision (imprecision). Investigate: pipetting technique/volume errors, reagent integrity (expiration, contamination, reconstitution), environmental factors (temperature, humidity), instrument components (sample probe, flow cell, light source). Must resolve before reporting patient results.

stem: A laboratory must determine staffing requirements for a new molecular diagnostics section. The workload analysis should be based on:
options: Number of employees in other departments
CAP workload recording method using test volumes, complexity, and time standards per procedure
rationale: CAP workload recording method (WRM) provides standardized time units per procedure, accounting for test complexity, specimen processing, QC, maintenance, and administrative tasks. Workload analysis: (test volume × time per test) + QC + maintenance + administrative time = total labor hours needed. Consider procedure complexity, turnaround time requirements, and coverage for 24/7 operations.

stem: Next-generation sequencing (NGS) of a tumor specimen reveals a BRAF V600E mutation. This finding is clinically significant because:
options: It is a normal variant found in all individuals
It indicates the tumor may respond to targeted therapy with BRAF inhibitors such as vemurafenib
It is relevant only for hereditary genetic counseling
rationale: BRAF V600E is an activating oncogenic mutation found in melanoma (~50%), colorectal cancer, thyroid cancer, and hairy cell leukemia. It drives constitutive MAPK pathway signaling. Targeted therapies: vemurafenib, dabrafenib (BRAF inhibitors) ± trametinib (MEK inhibitor). NGS enables comprehensive tumor profiling for precision medicine/companion diagnostics.

stem: A technologist sustains a needlestick injury from a needle used on an HIV-positive patient. The correct post-exposure protocol includes:
options: Washing with soap and water only and waiting for symptoms
Immediately wash the wound, report to supervisor, initiate post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) within 2 hours, baseline and follow-up testing at 6 weeks, 12 weeks, and 6 months
Apply a bandage and continue working
Test the technologist's blood only at 6 months
rationale: OSHA/CDC post-exposure protocol: (1) Immediately wash wound with soap and water, flush mucous membranes. (2) Report to supervisor and occupational health. (3) Evaluate exposure — source patient HIV status, viral load. (4) Start PEP (3-drug ART regimen) ideally within 2 hours, no later than 72 hours. (5) Baseline testing + follow-up at 6wk, 12wk, 6mo. Document in OSHA 301 log.

stem: A patient with sickle cell disease presents with acute chest pain. The CBC shows Hgb 62 g/L, reticulocyte count 18%, and the peripheral smear shows numerous sickle cells, target cells, and Howell-Jolly bodies. The Howell-Jolly bodies indicate:
options: Functional asplenia from repeated splenic infarction
rationale: Howell-Jolly bodies are nuclear remnants (DNA fragments) normally removed by splenic pitting function. In sickle cell disease, repeated splenic infarction leads to autosplenectomy (functional asplenia) by age 5. Absent splenic function → Howell-Jolly bodies, target cells, and increased infection risk (encapsulated organisms). Other causes: post-splenectomy, megaloblastic anemia.

stem: A patient's troponin I level is 0.08 ng/mL (reference: <0.04 ng/mL) at ED presentation and rises to 2.5 ng/mL at 6 hours. This pattern indicates:
options: Acute myocardial infarction with rising troponin pattern
rationale: Troponin I is cardiac-specific, released from damaged myocardium. A rise and/or fall pattern with at least one value above the 99th percentile URL (0.04 ng/mL) meets the biomarker criteria for acute MI (Type 1). Serial testing at 0, 3-6 hours detects the rise. Peak troponin correlates with infarct size. High-sensitivity troponin assays enable earlier detection.

stem: A blood culture bottle flags positive at 14 hours. The Gram stain shows Gram-positive cocci in chains. The organism grows on blood agar as small, translucent colonies with a large zone of beta-hemolysis. Bacitracin susceptible, PYR positive. The organism is:
rationale: S. pyogenes (GAS): Gram-positive cocci in chains, large zone of beta-hemolysis on blood agar, bacitracin susceptible (A disk), PYR-positive. Key differential: S. agalactiae (GBS) is bacitracin-resistant, PYR-negative, CAMP-positive. Enterococcus is PYR-positive but bile-esculin positive and grows in 6.5% NaCl. Lancefield grouping confirms Group A antigen.

stem: An antibody panel shows reactivity with all Jk(a+) cells and no reactivity with Jk(a-) cells at the antiglobulin phase. The antibody is anti-Jka. This antibody is clinically significant because:
options: It causes only in vitro reactions
It causes delayed hemolytic transfusion reactions and can show dosage effect — characteristically evanescent, falling below detectable levels between exposures
It is a cold antibody without clinical significance
It reacts only at room temperature
rationale: Anti-Jka (Kidd system): clinically significant IgG antibody causing delayed hemolytic transfusion reactions (DHTR) and hemolytic disease of the fetus/newborn. Characteristically evanescent — antibody titer drops below detectable levels, then rapidly reappears upon re-exposure (anamnestic response). Shows dosage (reacts stronger with homozygous Jk(a+b-) cells). Kidd antibodies activate complement.

stem: A patient's complement levels show: C3 decreased, C4 decreased, CH50 decreased, and elevated anti-dsDNA antibodies. The complement consumption pattern is most consistent with:
options: Systemic lupus erythematosus with classical pathway activation
rationale: Low C3, low C4, low CH50 = classical pathway activation consuming all complement components. In SLE, immune complexes (containing anti-dsDNA) activate C1q → classical pathway → consumption of C4 (classical pathway), C3 (common pathway), and total hemolytic complement (CH50). Alternative pathway activation: low C3, normal C4. Hereditary angioedema: low C4, normal C3 (C1-INH deficiency).

stem: A pleural fluid analysis shows: appearance turbid, WBC 12,500/μL (85% neutrophils), LDH 650 IU/L (serum LDH 200 IU/L), protein 5.2 g/dL (serum protein 7.0 g/dL), glucose 35 mg/dL, pH 7.10. Using Light's criteria, this fluid is classified as:
options: Exudate — meets all three Light's criteria with complicated parapneumonic features
rationale: Light's criteria for exudate (any one met): fluid/serum protein ratio >0.5 (5.2/7.0 = 0.74 ✓), fluid/serum LDH ratio >0.6 (650/200 = 3.25 ✓), fluid LDH >2/3 upper limit of normal serum LDH (✓). Additionally: low glucose (<60) and low pH (<7.20) indicate complicated parapneumonic effusion — likely needs drainage. Send for Gram stain and culture.

stem: When evaluating QC data, a systematic error (bias) is best detected by which statistical measure?
options: Mean comparison to the target value — persistent deviation from the accepted mean indicates bias
rationale: Bias (systematic error) = consistent deviation from the true/target value in one direction. Detected by comparing the observed mean to the accepted/target mean (accuracy assessment). SD and CV measure precision (random error), not accuracy. Bias sources: calibration error, deteriorated reagents, pipetting inaccuracy. Corrected by recalibration or reagent replacement. Bland-Altman plots and t-tests also assess bias.

stem: A hospital laboratory is evaluating whether to bring STAT troponin testing in-house versus sending it to a reference laboratory. The critical factor in this decision is:
options: Turnaround time — STAT troponin requires results within 60 minutes for clinical decision-making in acute coronary syndrome
The preference of the laboratory director alone
rationale: STAT troponin TAT goal: <60 minutes from specimen collection to result reporting (ACC/AHA guidelines). Reference lab send-out cannot meet this TAT for acute MI diagnosis. Cost analysis must include: reagent cost, instrument cost/lease, labor, QC, proficiency testing, maintenance — weighed against clinical impact of delayed diagnosis. In-house testing essential for ED-based ACS evaluation.

stem: A pharmacogenomic test reveals a patient is a CYP2C19 poor metabolizer (*2/*2 genotype). This result affects clinical management of:
options: Clopidogrel — poor metabolizers cannot convert the prodrug to its active metabolite, requiring alternative antiplatelet therapy
rationale: CYP2C19 metabolizes clopidogrel (prodrug) to its active antiplatelet metabolite. Poor metabolizers (*2/*2, *2/*3, *3/*3) have reduced/absent enzyme activity → inadequate platelet inhibition → increased risk of cardiovascular events. FDA boxed warning on clopidogrel. Alternatives: prasugrel, ticagrelor. Pharmacogenomics enables personalized antiplatelet therapy. CPIC guidelines direct clinical action.

stem: A laboratory technologist is working with Mycobacterium tuberculosis cultures in a BSL-3 laboratory. The minimum PPE requirements include:
options: N95 respirator (fit-tested), double gloves, solid-front gown, and work performed in a certified Class II or III biological safety cabinet
rationale: M. tuberculosis = BSL-3 organism (transmitted via aerosol). Requirements: N95 respirator (annual fit-testing per OSHA), double gloves, solid-front gown with tight wrists, work in certified Class II BSC (or Class III for high-risk procedures). BSL-3 facility: negative pressure, HEPA-filtered exhaust, controlled access, autoclave within facility. TB cultures are high-risk — manipulations can generate aerosols.

stem: A patient on warfarin therapy has an INR of 5.8. The PT is 58 seconds (reference 11-15 seconds). The mixing study shows correction to an INR of 1.2. This result indicates:
options: Factor deficiency corrected by normal plasma — consistent with warfarin effect on vitamin K-dependent factors
rationale: Mixing study principle: patient plasma mixed 1:1 with normal pooled plasma. Correction to normal = factor deficiency (factors supplied by normal plasma). Warfarin inhibits vitamin K-dependent factors (II, VII, IX, X, protein C/S) → prolonged PT/INR that corrects on mixing. Non-correction = factor inhibitor (lupus anticoagulant, specific factor inhibitor). INR 5.8 represents supratherapeutic anticoagulation — bleeding risk.

stem: A serum sample produces a milky, lipemic appearance. The triglyceride level is 1,850 mg/dL. This lipemia will most significantly interfere with which assay methodology?
options: Spectrophotometric assays — lipemia causes light scattering and falsely elevated absorbance readings
Blood gas analysis on whole blood
rationale: Lipemia causes turbidity → light scattering → falsely elevated absorbance in spectrophotometric assays (affects bilirubin, hemoglobin, LDH, CRP). Mitigation: ultracentrifugation to clear lipemia, use of sample blanking, or non-spectrophotometric methods. ISE for electrolytes is less affected (direct ISE). Severe lipemia (TG >1,000) can cause pseudohyponatremia with indirect ISE due to volume displacement.

stem: A stool culture on Hektoen enteric (HE) agar produces blue-green colonies with black centers. The organism is motile, produces H2S on TSI (alkaline/acid with gas and H2S), and is lysine decarboxylase positive. The most likely pathogen is:
rationale: Salmonella on HE agar: blue-green colonies with black centers (H2S production). TSI: K/A with gas and H2S (non-lactose fermenter, glucose fermenter, H2S producer). Lysine decarboxylase positive. Motile (peritrichous flagella). Shigella: no H2S, non-motile, lysine negative. Key differentials: Salmonella is H2S+, motile, lysine+; Shigella is H2S-, non-motile, lysine-. Serotyping determines serovar.

stem: A massive transfusion protocol (MTP) patient has received 12 units of RBCs in 6 hours. The laboratory should proactively prepare and issue:
options: Balanced ratio of RBCs, FFP, and platelets (1:1:1 ratio) to prevent dilutional coagulopathy, and monitor ionized calcium for citrate toxicity
rationale: Massive transfusion (>10 units RBCs in 24hr or >4 units in 1hr): 1:1:1 ratio of RBCs:FFP:platelets per damage control resuscitation guidelines. Complications: dilutional coagulopathy (depleted clotting factors and platelets), citrate toxicity (hypocalcemia — monitor ionized calcium), hypothermia (use blood warmer), hyperkalemia (stored RBC K+ leakage), and metabolic alkalosis (citrate → bicarbonate).

stem: A patient presents with recurrent sinopulmonary infections. Immunoglobulin levels show: IgG 180 mg/dL (reference 700-1600), IgA <7 mg/dL (reference 70-400), IgM 25 mg/dL (reference 40-230). The most likely diagnosis is:
rationale: CVID: significant reduction in IgG (at least 2 SD below mean) plus low IgA and/or IgM, with impaired vaccine responses. Most common symptomatic primary immunodeficiency in adults. Presents with recurrent sinopulmonary infections, autoimmune complications, and increased lymphoma risk. X-linked agammaglobulinemia (Bruton's): absent B cells, presents in infancy. Selective IgA deficiency: isolated low IgA with normal IgG/IgM.

stem: A urine specimen has a specific gravity of 1.035, pH 5.0, and microscopy reveals hexagonal crystals. These crystals are:
rationale: Cystine crystals: hexagonal (benzene-ring shaped), colorless, found in acidic urine. Pathognomonic for cystinuria — autosomal recessive disorder of renal tubular amino acid transport (cystine, ornithine, lysine, arginine — mnemonic COLA). Cystine stones are radiopaque, resistant to lithotripsy. Cyanide-nitroprusside test confirms cystine. Treatment: hydration, alkalinization (pH >7.5), D-penicillamine, or tiopronin.

stem: A laboratory calculates its total testing error (TEa) for HbA1c as 6% and its observed total error as 8.5%. According to Six Sigma methodology, the sigma metric for this assay is:
options: Greater than 6 sigma — world class performance
Less than 3 sigma — poor performance requiring increased QC frequency and process improvement
Cannot be calculated from this information
rationale: Sigma metric = (TEa - Bias) / CV. If total observed error (8.5%) exceeds the allowable total error (TEa = 6%), the process cannot meet quality goals — sigma <3 (unacceptable). Six Sigma quality levels: ≥6σ = world class (minimal QC needed), 5σ = excellent, 4σ = good, 3σ = minimum acceptable (requires strict QC), <3σ = poor (more QC, process improvement needed). HbA1c at this performance level needs immediate investigation.

stem: A laboratory director must justify the capital expense of a new automated blood culture system. The most appropriate approach is:
options: Prepare a cost-benefit analysis including improved detection rates, decreased time-to-positivity, labor savings, and impact on patient outcomes (reduced sepsis mortality and length of stay)
Compare prices from a single vendor
rationale: Capital equipment justification requires comprehensive cost-benefit analysis: direct costs (purchase/lease, reagents, maintenance contracts, training), indirect savings (labor reduction, improved TAT, decreased repeat testing), and clinical impact (faster sepsis detection → earlier targeted therapy → reduced mortality and hospital stay). ROI calculation, comparison of multiple vendors, and alignment with laboratory strategic plan are essential for administrative approval.

stem: Sanger sequencing of a BRCA1 gene reveals a heterozygous frameshift mutation c.5266dupC (5382insC). This result should be interpreted as:
options: Pathogenic variant — a well-characterized BRCA1 founder mutation associated with significantly increased breast and ovarian cancer risk, requiring genetic counseling referral
rationale: BRCA1 c.5266dupC (historically 5382insC) is a well-characterized pathogenic founder mutation prevalent in Ashkenazi Jewish populations. Frameshift → premature stop codon → truncated, non-functional BRCA1 protein → impaired DNA double-strand break repair. Lifetime breast cancer risk ~70%, ovarian cancer risk ~40%. Requires genetic counseling, cascade testing of at-risk relatives, and risk management discussion. ACMG classification: Pathogenic.

stem: A laboratory receives a chemical shipment. The SDS (Safety Data Sheet) for a new reagent indicates it is classified as GHS Category 1 acute toxicity (oral). The pictogram and precautions required are:
options: Skull and crossbones pictogram, danger signal word, store in locked cabinet, specific antidote information must be readily available, use in fume hood with full PPE
rationale: GHS Category 1 acute toxicity = most severe category (fatal if swallowed/inhaled/dermal contact). Requirements: skull and crossbones pictogram, DANGER signal word, SDS Section 4 (first aid with specific antidote), locked storage, inventory tracking, designated handling area with ventilation, full PPE (gloves, face shield, fume hood). All employees must be trained on hazards before use. Emergency procedures posted.

stem: A bone marrow aspirate shows hypercellularity with >20% myeloblasts that are positive for myeloperoxidase (MPO) stain, CD13, CD33, and CD117 by flow cytometry. Auer rods are present. The diagnosis is:
options: Chronic myelogenous leukemia in blast crisis
rationale: AML diagnostic criteria (WHO): ≥20% myeloblasts in bone marrow or blood. MPO positivity confirms myeloid lineage. CD13, CD33, CD117 = myeloid markers. Auer rods (fused azurophilic granules) are pathognomonic for myeloid neoplasms. Subtype classification requires cytogenetics and molecular studies — t(15;17) PML-RARA = APL (medical emergency requiring ATRA). Flow cytometry immunophenotyping essential for lineage assignment.

stem: A patient's metabolic panel shows Na+ 128 mEq/L, serum osmolality 260 mOsm/kg, urine osmolality 450 mOsm/kg, and urine sodium 45 mEq/L. The hyponatremia pattern is consistent with:
rationale: SIADH criteria: hyponatremia (Na+ <135), low serum osmolality (<280), inappropriately concentrated urine (urine osm >100, typically >300), elevated urine Na+ (>40 mEq/L), euvolemic status, normal thyroid and adrenal function. ADH is inappropriately secreted despite low serum osmolality → water retention → dilutional hyponatremia. Causes: CNS disorders, lung disease, medications (SSRIs, carbamazepine), malignancy.

stem: A throat culture on blood agar shows small colonies with a wide zone of beta-hemolysis. The organism is catalase-negative, PYR-positive, and bacitracin-susceptible. A rapid antigen detection test (RADT) is also positive. To confirm, the most definitive identification method is:
options: Latex agglutination for Lancefield Group A antigen
rationale: Lancefield serological grouping detects group-specific carbohydrate antigens on streptococcal cell wall. Group A = S. pyogenes. Presumptive ID: beta-hemolysis + PYR-positive + bacitracin-susceptible. Definitive ID: Lancefield grouping (latex agglutination or coagglutination). RADT detects Group A antigen directly from throat swab. Bile solubility and optochin are for S. pneumoniae (alpha-hemolytic).

stem: A neonate develops jaundice within 24 hours of birth. The mother is type O, Rh-positive, and the baby is type A, Rh-positive. The DAT on the baby's RBCs is weakly positive. The most likely cause is:
options: ABO hemolytic disease of the newborn — maternal anti-A IgG crossing the placenta and coating fetal A cells
rationale: ABO HDN: type O mother has naturally occurring anti-A and anti-B (IgG crosses placenta) → coats fetal A or B cells → mild hemolysis. DAT is often weakly positive or negative (fewer A/B antigen sites on neonatal RBCs). Jaundice within 24 hours = pathological (not physiological, which appears at 48-72 hours). ABO HDN is the most common cause of HDN but is usually milder than Rh HDN. Treatment: phototherapy, rarely exchange transfusion.

stem: A rapid plasma reagin (RPR) test is reactive at 1:64. The confirmatory FTA-ABS test is also reactive. Six months after treatment, the RPR titer drops to 1:4. This indicates:
options: Adequate serological response to treatment — a fourfold (two dilution) decrease in titer indicates successful therapy
rationale: Syphilis serology follow-up: successful treatment → fourfold decline in RPR/VDRL titer within 6-12 months (e.g., 1:64 → 1:16 or lower = adequate response). RPR 1:64 → 1:4 = four-tube (16-fold) decrease — excellent response. RPR/VDRL are non-treponemal tests that correlate with disease activity. FTA-ABS (treponemal test) usually remains reactive for life even after treatment. Inadequate response: <fourfold decline → possible treatment failure or reinfection.

stem: A 24-hour urine collection has a total volume of 2,800 mL. The creatinine concentration is 45 mg/dL and the serum creatinine is 1.8 mg/dL. The estimated creatinine clearance is:
rationale: Creatinine clearance = (Urine Cr × Volume) / (Serum Cr × Time). UCr = 45 mg/dL, V = 2800 mL/1440 min = 1.944 mL/min, PCr = 1.8 mg/dL. CrCl = (45 × 1.944) / 1.8 = 87.5 / 1.8 = 48.6 mL/min. This indicates decreased GFR (normal ~90-120 mL/min), consistent with impaired renal function.

stem: A laboratory is evaluating its proficiency testing (PT) results and finds that it failed the HbA1c PT challenge — the lab result was 7.8% while the target was 6.5%. The required corrective action includes:
options: No action needed for a single failure
Investigate root cause, document corrective action, retrain staff if needed, verify calibration and reagents, submit documentation to CAP/CMS within required timeframe
rationale: PT failure triggers mandatory corrective action under CLIA '88: (1) Investigate root cause (calibration, reagents, technique, instrument). (2) Implement corrective action. (3) Document all findings and actions. (4) Retrain personnel if competency issue identified. (5) Submit corrective action documentation. Two consecutive or two of three PT failures = unsuccessful participation → potential loss of certification for that analyte. Must cease patient testing until resolved.

stem: A laboratory information system (LIS) interface sends patient results electronically to the EMR. The data standard used for this electronic communication in healthcare is:
options: HL7 (Health Level 7) messaging standard, specifically HL7 v2.x for laboratory result transmission
rationale: HL7 v2.x is the standard for healthcare electronic data interchange, including laboratory results (OBR/OBX segments), orders (ORM messages), and ADT (admission/discharge/transfer). HL7 FHIR (Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources) is the newer RESTful API standard. LIS-EMR interfaces use HL7 messages transmitted via TCP/IP (MLLP). DICOM is for medical imaging. LOINC codes standardize laboratory test identification.

stem: A fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) test on bone marrow cells shows the BCR-ABL1 fusion signal in 85% of analyzed cells. This result is diagnostic for:
options: Chronic myelogenous leukemia (CML) — the BCR-ABL1 fusion gene results from t(9;22)(q34;q11) Philadelphia chromosome
rationale: BCR-ABL1 fusion gene results from t(9;22) Philadelphia chromosome — hallmark of CML (>95% of cases). FISH detects the fusion in interphase cells (doesn't require metaphases). The BCR-ABL1 tyrosine kinase is the therapeutic target for imatinib (Gleevec) and other TKIs. Monitoring by RT-qPCR (BCR-ABL1/ABL1 ratio) assesses treatment response. Also found in ~25% of adult ALL (Ph+ ALL). 85% positive cells indicates high disease burden.

stem: During a laboratory fire, a Class B fire extinguisher should be used for:
options: Flammable liquid fires (solvents, alcohols, oils, grease) — Class B covers burning liquids and gases
rationale: Fire extinguisher classes: A = ordinary combustibles (paper, wood, cloth), B = flammable liquids/gases (solvents, alcohols, grease, oils), C = electrical equipment (de-energize first if possible), D = combustible metals (lithium, magnesium, sodium), K = cooking oils/fats. Laboratories typically have ABC combination and CO2 extinguishers. PASS technique: Pull pin, Aim at base, Squeeze handle, Sweep side to side. Know extinguisher locations and evacuation routes.

stem: A reticulocyte count is 8% in a patient with Hgb 70 g/L. The corrected reticulocyte count (CRC) using a normal Hgb of 150 g/L is approximately:
options: 3.7% — CRC = reticulocyte % × (patient Hct/normal Hct), reflecting actual marrow response relative to anemia severity
rationale: CRC = reticulocyte % × (patient Hgb/normal Hgb) = 8% × (70/150) = 3.73%. The raw reticulocyte count overestimates marrow response in anemia because fewer circulating RBCs inflate the percentage. CRC >2% = adequate marrow response (hemolysis, blood loss). CRC <2% = inadequate response (marrow failure, nutritional deficiency). Reticulocyte production index (RPI) further corrects for shift reticulocytes.

stem: A patient's comprehensive metabolic panel shows AST 285 U/L, ALT 310 U/L, ALP 95 U/L, total bilirubin 2.8 mg/dL, direct bilirubin 1.9 mg/dL, albumin 3.8 g/dL. The pattern suggests:
options: Hepatocellular injury — elevated transaminases (AST/ALT) with relatively normal ALP indicates hepatocyte damage
rationale: Hepatocellular pattern: ALT > AST elevation (both >10× ULN), with relatively normal ALP. ALT is more liver-specific than AST. Elevated direct (conjugated) bilirubin indicates impaired hepatic excretion. Causes: viral hepatitis, drug-induced liver injury, ischemic hepatitis. Obstructive pattern: ALP and GGT markedly elevated with mild transaminase elevation. AST:ALT ratio >2:1 suggests alcoholic hepatitis.

stem: A sputum specimen shows numerous squamous epithelial cells (>25 per LPF) and few WBCs (<10 per LPF) on Gram stain. The appropriate action is:
options: Reject the specimen and request a new collection — excessive squamous epithelial cells indicate oropharyngeal contamination, not a lower respiratory tract specimen
rationale: Sputum quality assessment (Murray-Washington criteria): >25 squamous epithelial cells/LPF = oropharyngeal contamination → reject and recollect. Acceptable specimen: <10 squamous epithelial cells and >25 WBCs per LPF. Culturing contaminated specimens yields misleading results (oropharyngeal flora, not true pathogens). Exception: specimens for Legionella, mycobacteria, or fungi may be processed regardless of quality.

stem: A patient requiring a platelet transfusion is typed as A-positive. The blood bank has no type A platelets available. The most appropriate alternative is:
options: Withhold platelets until type A is available
Issue ABO-compatible platelets — type A or AB platelets preferred; if unavailable, any ABO type with consideration of anti-A/anti-B titer in the plasma
Issue type O whole blood instead
Issue type B platelets without concern
rationale: Platelet transfusion ABO compatibility: ABO-identical preferred (type A for type A patient). If unavailable, ABO-compatible (AB platelets are universal). Out-of-group platelets can be given but contain plasma with anti-A/anti-B that could cause hemolysis — check titer if using group O platelets (high-titer anti-A). Volume reduction removes most plasma. Rh-negative platelets preferred for Rh-negative females of childbearing age; give RhIG if Rh-positive platelets used.

stem: A monospot (heterophile antibody) test is negative in a 15-year-old with pharyngitis, lymphadenopathy, and atypical lymphocytes on the peripheral smear. The next serological test to order is:
options: Repeat monospot in one week only
EBV-specific antibody panel (VCA IgM, VCA IgG, EA IgG, EBNA IgG) to detect early or heterophile-negative infectious mononucleosis
rationale: Heterophile antibody (monospot) sensitivity is ~85% but can be negative early in disease or in children <4 years. EBV-specific antibody panel: VCA IgM (acute infection), VCA IgG (acute/past), EA IgG (acute/reactivation), EBNA IgG (past infection — appears weeks after onset). Acute primary EBV: VCA IgM+, VCA IgG+, EA IgG+/-, EBNA IgG-. If all EBV markers negative, consider CMV (heterophile-negative mono syndrome).

stem: A urinalysis dipstick is positive for blood (3+) but the microscopic examination shows 0-2 RBCs/HPF. The most likely explanation is:
options: Myoglobinuria or hemoglobinuria — the dipstick peroxidase reaction detects free hemoglobin and myoglobin, not just intact RBCs
rationale: Urine dipstick blood pad detects the peroxidase activity of hemoglobin — reacts with free hemoglobin (hemoglobinuria from intravascular hemolysis) and myoglobin (myoglobinuria from rhabdomyolysis) as well as intact RBC hemoglobin. Positive dipstick + no RBCs on microscopy = free hemoglobin or myoglobin. Differentiate: serum appearance (pink = hemolysis, clear = myoglobin), CK (markedly elevated in rhabdomyolysis), urine myoglobin test.

stem: A laboratory calculates the standard deviation index (SDI) for its glucose QC as +2.5. This indicates:
options: The laboratory's mean is within acceptable range
The laboratory's mean is 2.5 standard deviations above the peer group mean — indicating positive bias that needs investigation
rationale: SDI = (lab mean - peer group mean) / peer group SD. SDI of +2.5 = lab mean is 2.5 SD above peer mean — significant positive bias. Acceptable SDI: -1.0 to +1.0. SDI 1.0-2.0: marginal, investigate. SDI >2.0: unacceptable, immediate corrective action required. Sources of bias: calibration error, reagent lot variation, method difference. SDI enables inter-laboratory comparison using peer group statistics.

stem: A Point-of-Care Testing (POCT) program must comply with the same regulatory requirements as the central laboratory. The POCT coordinator's responsibilities include:
options: Ensuring operator competency assessment, QC performance, proficiency testing enrollment, procedure documentation, and compliance with CLIA and accreditation standards
Delegating all responsibilities to nursing staff
rationale: POCT coordinator (typically MT/MLS) responsibilities: operator training and competency assessment (initial + annual), QC program (per manufacturer and regulatory requirements), PT enrollment for all POCT analytes, procedure manual maintenance, instrument maintenance schedules, connectivity to LIS for result documentation, compliance with CLIA '88/CAP/Joint Commission standards. POCT must meet the same quality standards as central laboratory testing.

stem: A multiplex PCR respiratory panel detects influenza A, influenza B, RSV, SARS-CoV-2, and 15 other respiratory pathogens from a single nasopharyngeal swab. The advantage of multiplex over individual assays is:
options: Simultaneous detection of multiple targets from a single specimen with rapid turnaround, enabling comprehensive syndromic testing
Multiplex is always cheaper per test
rationale: Multiplex PCR panels (syndromic testing): detect multiple pathogens simultaneously from one specimen, one extraction, one run. Advantages: comprehensive differential diagnosis, reduced TAT (1-2 hours), less specimen volume needed, improved pathogen detection rates. Considerations: higher per-test cost, potential for detecting colonizers vs true pathogens, need for antimicrobial stewardship interpretation. FDA-cleared panels include FilmArray, ePlex, Verigene.

stem: A laboratory worker develops a skin rash after repeated exposure to latex gloves. The allergist confirms a Type I (IgE-mediated) latex allergy. The employer is required to:
options: Provide non-latex glove alternatives (nitrile, vinyl), remove powdered latex gloves from the work area, document the allergy in occupational health records, and modify the work environment
Ignore the allergy if symptoms are mild
Transfer the employee to a non-laboratory position
rationale: OSHA/ADA requirements for latex allergy: provide reasonable accommodation — non-latex alternatives (nitrile preferred), remove powdered latex gloves (powder carries latex proteins as airborne allergens), create latex-safe environment, document in occupational health records, train coworkers. Type I latex allergy: IgE-mediated, can progress to anaphylaxis. Type IV: delayed contact dermatitis. Both require accommodation. Cannot discriminate or terminate based on allergy.

stem: A D-dimer result is 4,250 ng/mL FEU (reference <500 ng/mL FEU) in a postoperative patient with sudden onset dyspnea and tachycardia. This result is most useful for:
options: The high negative predictive value of D-dimer makes it useful for exclusion — an elevated result is non-specific and requires imaging (CT pulmonary angiography) for diagnosis
rationale: D-dimer: fibrin degradation product, elevated in thrombosis (DVT/PE), DIC, post-surgery, inflammation, malignancy, pregnancy. High sensitivity/negative predictive value — normal D-dimer effectively excludes VTE in low-to-moderate pretest probability. Elevated D-dimer is non-specific and does NOT confirm PE — requires CT pulmonary angiography for diagnosis. Post-surgical patients often have elevated D-dimer, limiting clinical utility.

stem: A patient's serum shows a monoclonal spike (M-spike) on serum protein electrophoresis (SPEP) in the gamma region. The protein is quantified at 3.2 g/dL. Immunofixation electrophoresis (IFE) reveals IgG kappa. The next laboratory test to order is:
options: Serum free light chain ratio, 24-hour urine protein electrophoresis (UPEP) with immunofixation, beta-2 microglobulin, and bone marrow biopsy
rationale: M-spike ≥3.0 g/dL on SPEP with IgG kappa monoclonal protein = concerning for multiple myeloma. Workup: serum free light chains (kappa:lambda ratio), 24-hour UPEP/IFE (Bence Jones proteinuria), beta-2 microglobulin (prognostic marker), CBC (anemia), creatinine (renal function), calcium (hypercalcemia), LDH, albumin, bone marrow biopsy (plasma cell percentage), skeletal survey or PET/CT. CRAB criteria for symptomatic myeloma: Calcium, Renal, Anemia, Bone lesions.

stem: An anaerobic blood culture bottle grows a Gram-positive rod that produces a double zone of beta-hemolysis on blood agar, is lecithinase-positive on egg yolk agar, and shows a positive Nagler reaction. The organism is:
rationale: C. perfringens: Gram-positive, large, boxcar-shaped rod, double zone of beta-hemolysis (inner zone = theta toxin, outer zone = alpha toxin/lecithinase), lecithinase-positive on egg yolk agar (opalescence), Nagler reaction positive (lecithinase inhibited by C. perfringens antitoxin). Causes gas gangrene (myonecrosis), food poisoning, and septicemia. Non-motile (unusual for Clostridium). Grows rapidly — colonies visible in 6-12 hours.

stem: An elution study is performed on a newborn's DAT-positive RBCs. The eluate reacts with A1 cells and B cells but not O cells. The antibody in the eluate is:
options: Anti-A,B — a single antibody produced by group O individuals that reacts with both A and B antigens
rationale: Anti-A,B (also called anti-AB): a single IgG antibody produced by group O individuals that reacts with both A and B antigens — distinct from separate anti-A and anti-B. Crosses the placenta (IgG) and coats fetal A or B RBCs → positive DAT → ABO HDN. Elution recovers the antibody from RBC surface for identification. Reacts with A1, A2, B cells; non-reactive with O cells. This is the primary antibody causing ABO HDN.

stem: A patient has a positive direct Coombs test (DAT) with anti-IgG and anti-C3d. The most likely clinical scenario is:
options: Warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia (WAIHA) — IgG autoantibody coating RBCs with complement activation
rationale: DAT positive for both IgG and C3d: most commonly WAIHA. IgG autoantibody binds RBCs at 37°C → complement activation → C3d deposition. Mixed DAT (IgG + C3d) found in ~50% of WAIHA cases. Cold agglutinin disease: C3d only (IgM activates complement then dissociates). Drug-induced (hapten type): IgG only. The combination of IgG + C3d carries worse prognosis with more severe hemolysis.

stem: A peritoneal fluid from a patient with suspected spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP) shows WBC 850/μL with 350 neutrophils/μL. The albumin gradient (SAAG) is 1.8 g/dL. The interpretation is:
options: SBP is diagnosed — absolute neutrophil count ≥250/μL in ascitic fluid is diagnostic, regardless of culture result; SAAG ≥1.1 indicates portal hypertension
rationale: SBP diagnostic criteria: ascitic fluid absolute PMN count ≥250 cells/μL (350 PMN here → meets criteria). Culture may be negative in ~40% of cases (culture-negative neutrocytic ascites — still treated as SBP). SAAG ≥1.1 g/dL = portal hypertension (cirrhosis) as the cause of ascites. Empiric antibiotics (ceftriaxone or cefotaxime) started immediately after paracentesis, before culture results. Blood cultures also recommended.

stem: A laboratory's analytical measurement range (AMR) for potassium is 2.0-10.0 mEq/L. A patient result of 1.5 mEq/L should be reported as:
options: Below the analytical measurement range (<2.0 mEq/L) — results below AMR cannot be accurately quantified and must be flagged
rationale: Results below the AMR (lower limit of linearity) cannot be accurately quantified by the method. Must report as '<2.0 mEq/L' (below AMR). If a precise value is clinically needed, use an alternative method validated for lower concentrations. Results above AMR require dilution and re-measurement. AMR verification is part of method validation — must be verified or established for each analyte per CLIA requirements.

stem: A laboratory is implementing lean methodology. The first step in a lean project to reduce turnaround time for STAT testing is:
options: Map the current process using a value stream map to identify waste, bottlenecks, and non-value-added steps
rationale: Lean methodology: Value Stream Mapping (VSM) is the foundational tool — documents every step from test order to result delivery, identifying: value-added vs non-value-added steps, wait times, batching delays, transport waste, redundant processes. Eight wastes (DOWNTIME): Defects, Overproduction, Waiting, Non-utilized talent, Transportation, Inventory, Motion, Extra-processing. Only after mapping can targeted improvements be implemented (Kaizen events).

stem: Cell-free DNA (cfDNA) testing in a pregnant woman at 12 weeks gestation screens for fetal chromosomal abnormalities. The test reports a high risk for trisomy 21. The appropriate next step is:
options: Diagnose Down syndrome based on cfDNA alone
cfDNA is a screening test — a high-risk result requires confirmatory diagnostic testing by amniocentesis or chorionic villus sampling with karyotype analysis
rationale: Non-invasive prenatal testing (NIPT) using cfDNA is a screening test, not diagnostic. Sensitivity >99% for trisomy 21, but positive predictive value depends on maternal age and prevalence. False positives occur from confined placental mosaicism, maternal chromosomal abnormalities, or vanishing twin. Confirmatory diagnostic testing (amniocentesis at 15-20 wk or CVS at 10-13 wk) with karyotype or chromosomal microarray required before clinical decisions.

stem: A mercury thermometer breaks in the chemistry laboratory, spilling approximately 5 mL of mercury on the benchtop. The proper spill response is:
options: Wipe it up with paper towels and discard

Do not vacuum or sweep — use a mercury spill kit (aspirator bulb or specialized suction device), contain droplets with sulfur powder or zinc/sulfur amalgamation compound, ventilate the area, and dispose as hazardous waste
Leave it and open a window
rationale: Mercury spill protocol: (1) Evacuate immediate area if large spill. (2) Do NOT vacuum (disperses mercury vapor) or sweep (breaks into smaller droplets). (3) Use mercury spill kit: aspirator bulb or commercial mercury vacuum to collect droplets, sulfur powder or zinc amalgamation powder to bind residual mercury. (4) Ventilate area. (5) Place all contaminated materials in sealed container labeled as hazardous waste. (6) Monitor with mercury vapor detector. Mercury is a chronic toxicant — CNS, renal, respiratory effects.

stem: A patient's peripheral blood shows schistocytes, thrombocytopenia (PLT 35 x10^9/L), elevated LDH (1,250 U/L), decreased haptoglobin (<10 mg/dL), elevated indirect bilirubin, and a negative DAT. The reticulocyte count is 12%. This presentation is consistent with:
options: Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP) — microangiopathic hemolytic anemia with thrombocytopenia
rationale: TTP classic pentad: microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (MAHA — schistocytes, elevated LDH, low haptoglobin, elevated indirect bilirubin, negative DAT), thrombocytopenia, fever, renal dysfunction, neurological symptoms. MAHA + thrombocytopenia is sufficient to initiate treatment. Caused by ADAMTS13 deficiency → uncleaved ultra-large vWF multimers → platelet microthrombi. Plasma exchange is life-saving. Check ADAMTS13 activity before treatment if possible.

stem: A patient's BNP (B-type natriuretic peptide) level is 1,450 pg/mL (reference <100 pg/mL). This result is most useful in evaluating:
options: Heart failure — BNP is released from ventricular myocytes in response to volume overload and wall stress
rationale: BNP/NT-proBNP: released from ventricular cardiomyocytes in response to increased wall stress (volume/pressure overload). BNP >400 pg/mL strongly suggests heart failure. Used for diagnosis, severity assessment, and treatment monitoring. BNP <100 pg/mL effectively excludes HF (high negative predictive value). Elevated in: HF, renal failure, PE, sepsis. Reduced by obesity (adipose tissue clears BNP). NT-proBNP has longer half-life and higher values.

stem: A laboratory isolates a yeast from a blood culture that produces germ tubes when incubated in serum at 37°C for 2-3 hours. The yeast also forms chlamydospores on cornmeal agar. The organism is:
rationale: C. albicans identification: germ tube test positive (>95% specific — short hyphal extension from yeast cell in serum at 37°C), chlamydospore production on cornmeal-Tween 80 agar (large, thick-walled terminal spores). C. dubliniensis is the only other germ tube-positive species but is rare. C. glabrata: no germ tubes, no pseudohyphae. C. auris: difficult to identify by conventional methods, requires MALDI-TOF or molecular ID. Candida is the #4 cause of bloodstream infections.

stem: A transfusion reaction investigation reveals: pre-transfusion specimen is type A-positive, post-transfusion specimen shows mixed-field agglutination with anti-B, and the transfused unit was type AB-positive. The investigation conclusion is:
options: Wrong blood type transfused — the patient is type A and received type AB blood, introducing B-antigen-positive donor cells detected as mixed-field agglutination
rationale: Mixed-field agglutination with anti-B in a type A patient post-transfusion = circulating donor B-antigen-positive RBCs mixed with patient's A-positive RBCs. Type A patient has anti-B in their plasma → hemolysis of transfused B-antigen cells. This is an ABO-incompatible transfusion error. Investigate: clerical check (patient ID, unit labels, crossmatch records), hemolysis markers (LDH, haptoglobin, bilirubin, DAT, plasma/urine free hemoglobin), renal function monitoring.

stem: A patient's HIV testing algorithm shows: 4th generation combo assay (Ag/Ab) reactive, HIV-1/HIV-2 differentiation assay reactive for HIV-1, HIV-1 RNA viral load 45,000 copies/mL. According to the CDC-recommended algorithm, the interpretation is:
options: Confirmed HIV-1 infection — the current CDC algorithm uses 4th gen screening, supplemental differentiation assay, and nucleic acid testing when needed
rationale: CDC/APHL recommended HIV testing algorithm (2014): (1) 4th generation Ag/Ab combo assay (detects p24 Ag + HIV-1/2 Ab). (2) If reactive → HIV-1/HIV-2 differentiation immunoassay. (3) If differentiation is positive for HIV-1 → confirmed HIV-1 infection. HIV-1 RNA confirms and provides baseline viral load. Western blot is no longer recommended as confirmatory. If differentiation is negative/indeterminate → HIV-1 RNA (NAT) to detect acute infection.

stem: A semen analysis shows: volume 3.5 mL, sperm concentration 8 million/mL, total motility 25%, morphology 2% normal forms (strict Kruger criteria). The interpretation per WHO 2021 criteria is:
options: Oligoasthenoteratozoospermia (OAT) — decreased count (<16 million/mL), decreased motility (<42%), and decreased morphology (<4% normal)
rationale: WHO 2021 lower reference limits: volume ≥1.4 mL, concentration ≥16 million/mL, total motility ≥42%, progressive motility ≥30%, morphology ≥4% normal (strict criteria). This sample: oligozoospermia (8M < 16M), asthenozoospermia (25% < 42%), teratozoospermia (2% < 4%). Combined = OAT syndrome. Abstinence 2-7 days before collection. Repeat in 2-3 months for confirmation (spermatogenesis cycle ~74 days).

stem: A laboratory receives a new lot of reagent for its glucose assay. Before placing it in service, the technologist must perform:
options: No verification needed for FDA-cleared reagents
New lot verification — run both old and new lot in parallel with QC and patient samples to ensure comparable results before switching
Return the old lot to the manufacturer
rationale: Reagent lot-to-lot verification: run QC materials (at minimum two levels) and parallel patient samples on both old and new lots. Results must be within acceptable limits before new lot enters service. Document: lot numbers, expiration dates, QC results, comparison data. Per CAP/CLIA: unverified reagent lots cannot be used for patient testing. This prevents systematic errors from lot-to-lot variation in reagent concentration, calibrator values, or antibody specificity.

stem: A laboratory's employee competency assessment program must include evaluation at which intervals per CLIA regulations?
options: At hire, 6 months after hire, and annually thereafter — competency must be assessed using at least 6 defined elements
rationale: CLIA '88 competency assessment: at hire (orientation), 6 months post-hire, and annually thereafter. Six required elements: (1) direct observation of test performance, (2) monitoring recording and reporting of results, (3) review of intermediate test results/worksheets, (4) direct observation of instrument maintenance, (5) assessment of test performance through PT or alternative assessment, (6) evaluation of problem-solving skills. Documentation must be maintained.

stem: Microsatellite instability (MSI) testing on a colorectal cancer specimen shows instability at 4 out of 5 NCI-recommended markers. This MSI-High result indicates:
options: Deficient DNA mismatch repair (dMMR) — associated with Lynch syndrome or sporadic MLH1 promoter methylation, qualifying the patient for immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy
rationale: MSI-H (≥2/5 NCI markers unstable or ≥30% of extended panel): indicates deficient mismatch repair (dMMR). Causes: Lynch syndrome (germline mutations in MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2) or sporadic MLH1 promoter methylation. Clinical significance: improved prognosis in stage II CRC, poor response to 5-FU, excellent response to immune checkpoint inhibitors (pembrolizumab FDA-approved for MSI-H/dMMR tumors regardless of site). Reflex to IHC for MMR proteins and MLH1 methylation testing.

stem: During an OSHA inspection, the inspector finds that the laboratory's Chemical Hygiene Plan (CHP) has not been updated in 4 years and several new chemicals have been added without risk assessments. The citation will be for violation of:
options: OSHA Laboratory Standard (29 CFR 1910.1450) — requiring a current, comprehensive Chemical Hygiene Plan addressing all chemical hazards in the laboratory
rationale: OSHA Laboratory Standard (29 CFR 1910.1450) requires: Chemical Hygiene Plan (CHP) that is current, reviewed annually, and addresses all chemicals in use. CHP must include: standard operating procedures for chemical handling, exposure determination and monitoring, PPE requirements, training, medical consultation provisions, and designation of a Chemical Hygiene Officer. Adding new chemicals requires risk assessment, SDS availability, and CHP update. Non-compliance: OSHA citations and fines.

stem: A flow cytometry panel on a bone marrow shows cells positive for CD10, CD19, CD20dim, CD34, and TdT. These markers are consistent with:
options: B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia/lymphoma (B-ALL) — the CD19/CD10/CD34/TdT phenotype indicates B-cell precursor origin
rationale: B-ALL immunophenotype: CD19+ (B-cell lineage), CD10+ (CALLA — common ALL antigen), CD34+ (immature/blast marker), TdT+ (terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase — marker of lymphoid precursors), dim CD20. This is the 'common' B-ALL phenotype, the most frequent subtype. CLL: CD5+, CD23+, CD19+, CD20dim, CD10-, TdT-. Hairy cell: CD19+, CD20bright, CD11c+, CD25+, CD103+. Cytogenetics critical: t(12;21) favorable, t(9;22) poor prognosis.

stem: A patient's lipid panel shows: total cholesterol 285 mg/dL, HDL 35 mg/dL, LDL 195 mg/dL, triglycerides 275 mg/dL. The LDL was calculated using the Friedewald equation. At what triglyceride level does this equation become unreliable?
options: Above 400 mg/dL — the Friedewald equation (LDL = TC - HDL - TG/5) is inaccurate when triglycerides exceed 400 mg/dL
rationale: Friedewald equation: LDL = Total Cholesterol - HDL - (Triglycerides/5). Valid only when TG <400 mg/dL (chylomicrons absent, fasting specimen). At TG ≥400, VLDL cholesterol estimation (TG/5) is inaccurate → unreliable LDL calculation. Alternatives: direct LDL measurement (homogeneous assay), Martin-Hopkins equation (variable TG:VLDL ratio), or ultracentrifugation (reference method). This patient's TG 275 is within valid range for Friedewald calculation.

stem: A sputum specimen submitted for acid-fast bacilli (AFB) testing shows rare acid-fast bacilli on concentrated auramine-rhodamine fluorochrome stain. The next step is:
options: Report as positive for AFB and wait for culture

Confirm with carbol fuchsin (Ziehl-Neelsen or Kinyoun) stain, report smear result with quantitation, inoculate culture media (both solid Lowenstein-Jensen and liquid MGIT), and order nucleic acid amplification test (NAAT)
Report as negative since only rare organisms seen
rationale: AFB smear-positive specimens: confirm fluorochrome positives with carbol fuchsin stain (higher specificity). Report quantitation per laboratory protocol (rare, few, moderate, many). Culture on solid (LJ, 7H11) and liquid (MGIT 960) media — liquid culture has faster detection (1-2 weeks vs 3-8 weeks). NAAT (GeneXpert MTB/RIF, Hologic Amplified MTD) provides rapid species identification and rifampin resistance in hours. Report smear results urgently — initiates airborne isolation.

stem: A patient with a history of anti-Fya requires a transfusion. The antigen frequency of Fy(a-) in the Caucasian population is approximately 34%. If the blood bank has 20 units available, approximately how many would be expected to be compatible?
options: Approximately 7 units (34% of 20) — antigen-negative units must be confirmed by phenotyping or genotyping before crossmatch
rationale: Anti-Fya is a clinically significant Duffy system antibody (IgG, causes DHTR and HDN). Fy(a-) frequency: ~34% Caucasian, ~90% African American (due to Duffy-null phenotype Fy(a-b-)). For 20 Caucasian-donor units: ~34% × 20 = ~7 antigen-negative units expected. Must antigen-type units before crossmatch. If more units needed: check inventory at blood supplier, use molecular genotyping for rare phenotypes. Always crossmatch antigen-negative units at AHG phase.

stem: A hepatitis B serological panel shows: HBsAg negative, anti-HBs positive (>100 mIU/mL), anti-HBc negative, HBeAg negative. This pattern indicates:
options: Immunity due to vaccination — anti-HBs positive without anti-HBc indicates vaccine-induced immunity, not natural infection
rationale: HBV serology interpretation: (1) Vaccine immunity: anti-HBs+ only (no anti-HBc, since core antigen not in vaccine). (2) Natural immunity (past infection): anti-HBs+ AND anti-HBc+. (3) Acute infection: HBsAg+, anti-HBc IgM+, HBeAg+/-. (4) Chronic infection: HBsAg+ >6 months, anti-HBc IgG+. (5) Window period: HBsAg-, anti-HBs-, anti-HBc IgM+. Anti-HBs ≥10 mIU/mL = protective; >100 indicates robust immunity.

stem: A fecal occult blood test (FOBT) using guaiac methodology shows a positive result. The patient was taking vitamin C supplements and ate red meat the day before collection. The most likely source of false-positive or false-negative result is:
options: Vitamin C (ascorbic acid) can cause false-negative results by inhibiting the peroxidase reaction, while red meat (animal hemoglobin/myoglobin) can cause false-positive results
rationale: Guaiac FOBT: detects peroxidase activity of hemoglobin. False positives: red meat (animal hemoglobin/myoglobin), raw vegetables with peroxidase activity (turnips, horseradish, broccoli), iron supplements. False negatives: vitamin C (>250 mg/day — reduces the oxidized guaiac, inhibiting color development). Dietary restrictions 3 days before collection. FIT (fecal immunochemical test) is specific for human hemoglobin — no dietary restrictions needed, preferred for CRC screening.

stem: During a method comparison study between two glucose analyzers, the Bland-Altman plot shows a positive bias of +8 mg/dL with 95% of differences falling between -2 and +18 mg/dL. The interpretation is:
options: The new method has a consistent positive bias of 8 mg/dL compared to the reference method — clinical acceptability depends on whether this bias is within the total allowable error for glucose
Random error is the primary concern
rationale: Bland-Altman analysis: plots the difference between two methods against their average. Mean difference (bias) = +8 mg/dL (systematic error — new method reads 8 mg/dL higher). Limits of agreement (mean ± 1.96 SD) = -2 to +18 mg/dL. Clinical acceptability: compare bias to TEa for glucose (CAP/CLIA: ±6 mg/dL or ±10%, whichever is greater). An 8 mg/dL bias may or may not be acceptable depending on the glucose concentration range.

stem: A laboratory is selecting a new hematology analyzer with pre-analytical sample handling capability. The most important factor for high-volume laboratories is:
options: Throughput capacity, automated sample loading with cap-piercing technology, auto-verification rules, and bidirectional LIS interface for efficient workflow
rationale: High-volume hematology analyzer selection: throughput (samples/hour), cap-piercing (biosafety, reduced handling), auto-verification (rules-based release of normal results — reduces manual review), bidirectional LIS interface (automated order download and result upload), flagging algorithms (sensitivity/specificity for abnormal cells), reflex testing capability (automated slide making/staining), and maintenance requirements. Total cost of ownership includes reagents, controls, service contracts, and labor savings.

stem: A tumor specimen tested by immunohistochemistry shows loss of MLH1 and PMS2 protein expression, with intact MSH2 and MSH6. Reflex testing for MLH1 promoter methylation is negative. The implication is:
options: Likely Lynch syndrome — MLH1/PMS2 loss without MLH1 methylation suggests germline MLH1 mutation, requiring genetic counseling and germline testing
rationale: MMR IHC showing MLH1/PMS2 loss: PMS2 loss is secondary (heterodimerizes with MLH1). If MLH1 promoter methylation positive → sporadic (somatic silencing). If methylation negative → likely germline MLH1 mutation (Lynch syndrome). Lynch syndrome: autosomal dominant, increased risk of CRC, endometrial, ovarian, urinary tract, and other cancers. Requires germline genetic testing and cascade testing of family members. Universal MMR/MSI testing recommended for all CRC per NCCN guidelines.

stem: A laboratory worker is splashed in the face with a concentrated sodium hydroxide (NaOH) solution. The immediate first aid action is:
options: Apply neutralizing acid to the affected area
Immediately flush eyes and skin with copious amounts of water at an emergency eyewash station for at least 15-20 minutes — do NOT attempt to neutralize the chemical
rationale: Chemical splash (caustic/corrosive): immediate copious water irrigation for minimum 15-20 minutes at emergency eyewash/safety shower. Do NOT neutralize (neutralization reaction generates heat → additional thermal burn). Remove contaminated clothing during irrigation. For eye exposure: hold eyelids open, irrigate continuously. After irrigation: seek medical evaluation. OSHA requires eyewash stations within 10 seconds travel distance from corrosive chemical use areas. Document incident.

stem: A CBC on an automated analyzer flags for platelet clumping. The EDTA blood sample shows platelet aggregates on the peripheral smear. The most appropriate corrective action is:
options: Report the low platelet count as is

Collect a new specimen in sodium citrate tube, perform platelet count, and multiply by 1.1 to correct for the 9:1 dilution factor — EDTA-dependent pseudothrombocytopenia requires alternative anticoagulant
rationale: EDTA-dependent pseudothrombocytopenia: EDTA-induced platelet clumping (anti-platelet antibodies activated by EDTA) → falsely low automated platelet count. Incidence: 0.1-0.2%. Solution: collect in sodium citrate (light blue top), count platelets, multiply by 1.1 (correction for 9:1 blood:citrate ratio). Alternative: run within 1 hour of collection, use kanamycin-EDTA, or ammonia oxalate manual count. Always check smear when platelet count is flagged.

stem: A patient's ammonia level drawn from an antecubital vein is reported as 185 μmol/L (reference 11-35 μmol/L). The specimen was received 2 hours after collection and was not placed on ice. The most appropriate action is:
options: Request a new specimen collected properly — ammonia specimens must be transported on ice, centrifuged and tested within 15 minutes; the delay likely caused falsely elevated results from in vitro amino acid deamination
rationale: Ammonia is extremely labile: in vitro deamination of amino acids by blood cell enzymes increases ammonia levels 20% within 1 hour at room temperature. Collection requirements: free-flowing venous or arterial blood, avoid tourniquet clenching, immediately place on ice, transport to lab immediately, centrifuge within 15 minutes, test plasma promptly. A 2-hour delay at room temperature renders the result unreliable. Must recollect following proper protocol.
