Advantages: broad-spectrum pathogen inactivation, replaces gamma irradiation (prevents TA-GVHD), reduces bacterial contamination testing need. Limitations: does not eliminate prions, may reduce platelet function slightly, additive cost. FDA-approved for platelets; whole blood/plasma pathogen reduction also available in some regions.

stem: A patient is being evaluated for antiphospholipid syndrome. The laboratory panel includes lupus anticoagulant (LA) testing, anticardiolipin antibodies (aCL), and anti-beta-2 glycoprotein I antibodies. The LA testing algorithm should include:
options: Two screening tests based on different principles (dRVVT and PTT-LA), mixing studies if screening is prolonged, and confirmatory testing with excess phospholipid to demonstrate phospholipid dependence
rationale: Lupus anticoagulant testing (ISTH guidelines): (1) Screen with two phospholipid-dependent clotting assays — dRVVT (dilute Russell viper venom time) AND aPTT-based test (PTT-LA, silica clotting time). (2) If prolonged → mixing study (fails to correct = inhibitor present, correction = factor deficiency). (3) Confirm phospholipid dependence — add excess phospholipid → shortening confirms LA. Must test on two occasions ≥12 weeks apart for APS diagnosis. Anticoagulant therapy interferes — DOACs especially affect dRVVT.

stem: A 24-hour urine collection for cortisol shows a result of 450 μg/24hr (reference 10-100 μg/24hr). This finding supports a diagnosis of:
options: Cushing's syndrome — markedly elevated urinary free cortisol indicates hypercortisolism, requiring confirmatory testing with dexamethasone suppression test and midnight salivary cortisol
rationale: Urinary free cortisol (UFC): 24-hour urine measures unbound cortisol filtered by the kidneys. UFC >3-4× ULN is virtually diagnostic of Cushing's syndrome. Screening algorithm: at least 2 of 3 tests should be abnormal — UFC, late-night salivary cortisol, 1 mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test. After confirming hypercortisolism: determine source — ACTH level (low = adrenal, elevated = pituitary or ectopic), high-dose dexamethasone suppression, inferior petrosal sinus sampling. Ensure complete 24-hour collection (check creatinine excretion for adequacy).

stem: A laboratory's external quality assessment (EQA) results for hemoglobin determination consistently fall 1.5 g/dL below the consensus mean over three consecutive surveys. This systematic bias requires:
options: No action since individual results are within 2 SD

Investigation for consistent negative bias — evaluate calibration, reagent integrity, sample handling, and compare with a reference method; persistent bias in EQA indicates the laboratory's results may be clinically inaccurate
rationale: Consistent EQA bias (3 consecutive surveys below consensus mean): systematic error pattern requiring investigation. Steps: (1) Verify calibration status and calibrator values. (2) Check reagent lot, stability, reconstitution. (3) Review maintenance records. (4) Compare with alternative method or reference laboratory. (5) Evaluate pre-analytical variables (specimen handling for EQA materials). (6) Consider matrix effects (EQA material vs patient samples). (7) Implement corrective action and document. Persistent bias of 1.5 g/dL can lead to clinical mismanagement.

stem: A laboratory is transitioning from manual differential counts to digital morphology systems (e.g., CellaVision). The validation of the digital system requires:
options: Visual inspection of a few slides

Comparison of digital pre-classification with manual microscopy on at least 100-200 patient samples covering normal and abnormal morphology, with evaluation of sensitivity and specificity for clinically significant cell types and abnormalities
rationale: Digital morphology validation: compare automated pre-classification vs expert manual review on diverse patient population. Minimum 100-200 slides including: normal differentials, left shifts, blast populations, abnormal morphology (sickle cells, schistocytes, parasites), nucleated RBCs. Evaluate: WBC pre-classification accuracy by cell type, flagging sensitivity for abnormals, morphology recognition. Must establish review criteria — which pre-classified results require technologist verification. Document competency for users. Monitor post-implementation discordance rates.

stem: A molecular laboratory detects a FLT3-ITD (internal tandem duplication) mutation in an AML patient's bone marrow sample. The allelic ratio is 0.8. The significance of this finding is:
options: Adverse prognosis — FLT3-ITD with high allelic ratio (≥0.5) is an adverse prognostic marker in AML, indicating the need for FLT3 inhibitor therapy (midostaurin, gilteritinib) in addition to standard chemotherapy
rationale: FLT3-ITD: most common molecular abnormality in AML (~25-30%). Internal tandem duplication of the juxtamembrane domain → constitutive activation of FLT3 receptor tyrosine kinase. Prognostic significance depends on allelic ratio: high ratio (≥0.5) = adverse prognosis, low ratio (<0.5) = intermediate. ELN 2022 classification: FLT3-ITD is adverse risk when allelic ratio is high, modifiable by NPM1 co-mutation. Targeted therapy: midostaurin (with induction chemo), gilteritinib (relapsed/refractory). Molecular MRD monitoring by qPCR.

stem: A laboratory's waste management program classifies waste into categories. Agar plates from microbiology with bacterial cultures are classified as:
options: Regulated medical waste (biohazardous/infectious waste) — requires autoclaving or other approved decontamination method before disposal, with proper packaging, labeling, and manifest documentation
rationale: Microbiology culture plates = regulated medical waste (infectious waste): contain viable pathogenic organisms. Disposal requirements: (1) Autoclave (121°C, 15-30 psi, minimum 30 minutes — validated with biological indicators). (2) OR chemical decontamination (10% bleach submersion). (3) Package in biohazard bags/containers (red bags or containers with biohazard symbol). (4) If autoclaved on-site → can go to regular landfill after decontamination. (5) If not treated on-site → licensed medical waste hauler with manifest tracking. State regulations vary — follow the most stringent requirement.

stem: A peripheral blood smear shows numerous teardrop cells (dacrocytes), nucleated red blood cells, and immature granulocytes (myelocytes, metamyelocytes) in a patient with splenomegaly. This leukoerythroblastic picture suggests:
options: Normal bone marrow response to infection
Myelofibrosis or bone marrow infiltrative process — the leukoerythroblastic pattern with dacrocytes indicates marrow space-occupying pathology forcing hematopoietic elements into the peripheral blood
rationale: Leukoerythroblastic blood picture: nucleated RBCs + immature WBCs (left shift to myelocytes/metamyelocytes) in peripheral blood. Indicates bone marrow infiltration or replacement. Dacrocytes (teardrop cells): RBCs squeezed through fibrotic marrow. Classic for: primary myelofibrosis (PMF), metastatic cancer to bone marrow, granulomatous disease, severe marrow stress. PMF: JAK2, CALR, or MPL mutations. Splenomegaly from extramedullary hematopoiesis. Bone marrow biopsy shows reticulin/collagen fibrosis (dry tap on aspiration).

stem: A patient's ABG shows pH 7.52, pCO2 28 mmHg, HCO3- 22 mEq/L, pO2 98 mmHg. The acid-base disorder is:
options: Respiratory alkalosis, acute (uncompensated) — the low pCO2 with alkaline pH and normal HCO3- indicates hyperventilation without metabolic compensation
rationale: pH >7.45 = alkalosis. Low pCO2 (28, normal 35-45) = respiratory cause (hyperventilation blowing off CO2). HCO3- is normal (22, normal 22-26) = no metabolic compensation yet = acute respiratory alkalosis. If compensated: HCO3- would decrease (renal compensation takes 2-5 days). Causes: anxiety/hyperventilation, hypoxia-driven tachypnea, pain, sepsis (early), PE, pregnancy, CNS disorders, salicylate toxicity (early). Expected compensation: HCO3- decreases ~2 mEq/L for each 10 mmHg decrease in pCO2.

stem: A chromogenic agar plate for MRSA screening shows mauve/pink colonies after 24 hours of incubation. This chromogenic method detects MRSA by:
options: Using a chromogenic substrate that is cleaved by an enzyme specific to S. aureus, combined with cefoxitin or oxacillin in the medium to select for methicillin resistance — allowing direct MRSA detection from clinical specimens
rationale: Chromogenic MRSA agar: contains (1) antibiotic (cefoxitin or oxacillin) — selects for methicillin-resistant organisms only, (2) chromogenic substrate — cleaved by S. aureus-specific enzyme (e.g., phosphatase) producing colored colonies (mauve, pink, or green depending on brand), (3) selective agents — inhibit other organisms. Advantages: direct from clinical specimen (nares swab), 24-hour results, no need for separate SA identification and susceptibility testing. Sensitivity ~90-95%, specificity >95%. Faster than culture + ID + susceptibility but slower than PCR.

stem: A blood bank must select appropriate units for a patient with anti-c and anti-K antibodies. The probability of finding c-negative, K-negative random donor units in the Caucasian population is approximately:
options: 16% — calculated by multiplying the frequency of c-negative (~18%) by K-negative (~91%): 0.18 × 0.91 ≈ 0.16 or 16%
rationale: Multiple antibody antigen-negative unit calculations: multiply the frequency of each negative antigen. c-negative frequency (Caucasian): ~18% (c antigen prevalence ~80%, so c-negative = ~20%; more precisely ~18%). K-negative frequency: ~91% (K prevalence ~9%). Probability of both: 0.18 × 0.91 = 0.164 (16.4%). For 6 units needed: 6/0.164 ≈ 37 units to screen. Molecular genotyping of donor units can expedite identification of antigen-negative donors for patients with multiple antibodies.

stem: A laboratory runs a correlation study between its POCT glucose meter and central laboratory glucose analyzer. The results show a Pearson correlation coefficient (r) of 0.98. However, this does NOT necessarily mean the methods agree. The limitation of correlation coefficient in method comparison is:
options: r = 0.98 proves the methods are interchangeable

Correlation measures the strength of linear relationship but does NOT assess agreement — two methods can be highly correlated but have significant proportional or constant bias; Bland-Altman analysis is needed to assess agreement
rationale: Correlation coefficient limitations for method comparison: (1) High r only shows linear relationship, not agreement (Y = 2X has r = 1.0 but 100% proportional bias). (2) r is affected by data range — wider range inflates r. (3) Does not identify constant or proportional bias. Proper method comparison: Deming or Passing-Bablok regression (slope = proportional bias, intercept = constant bias) AND Bland-Altman analysis (mean difference = bias, limits of agreement = precision of differences). TEa comparison determines clinical acceptability.

stem: A laboratory is implementing total quality management (TQM). The Deming cycle used for continuous quality improvement consists of:
options: Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) — a systematic iterative approach where improvements are planned, implemented, evaluated for effectiveness, and standardized or revised
rationale: PDCA (Deming cycle): Plan — identify the problem, analyze root causes, develop improvement plan. Do — implement the plan on a small scale (pilot). Check — evaluate results against expected outcomes using data. Act — if successful, standardize and expand; if not, revise the plan and repeat the cycle. Continuous iterative improvement. TQM principles: customer focus (patient, provider), process improvement, data-driven decisions, employee empowerment, teamwork. Quality tools: Pareto charts, fishbone diagrams, flowcharts, control charts, FMEA.

stem: A laboratory is validating a new CRISPR-Cas12a-based diagnostic assay for rapid SARS-CoV-2 detection. This emerging technology works by:
options: Target-specific guide RNA directs Cas12a to the viral sequence; upon binding, Cas12a exhibits collateral (trans) cleavage activity that cuts fluorescent reporter probes, generating a detectable signal — enabling isothermal, rapid, point-of-care diagnostics
Culturing the virus in cell culture
rationale: CRISPR diagnostics (DETECTR, SHERLOCK): (1) Isothermal amplification (RPA or LAMP) of target sequence. (2) Cas12a (DETECTR) or Cas13a (SHERLOCK) programmed with guide RNA recognizes the specific sequence. (3) Upon target binding, Cas enzyme activates collateral cleavage — non-specifically cuts single-stranded DNA (Cas12a) or RNA (Cas13a) reporter probes. (4) Cleaved reporters generate fluorescent or lateral flow signal. Advantages: isothermal (no thermocycler), rapid (<1 hour), high specificity (guide RNA), potential for POC format, multiplexable. FDA EUA granted for some CRISPR diagnostics.

stem: An ergonomic assessment of the laboratory reveals that technologists performing repetitive pipetting tasks report wrist pain and numbness. The condition most likely developing is:
options: Repetitive strain injury (RSI)/carpal tunnel syndrome — repetitive wrist motions from manual pipetting, combined with static postures, cause median nerve compression and tendon inflammation
rationale: Laboratory ergonomic injuries: repetitive pipetting → carpal tunnel syndrome (median nerve compression at wrist), trigger finger, de Quervain's tenosynovitis, epicondylitis. Prevention: ergonomic pipettes (reduced thumb force, electronic pipettes), proper workstation height, neutral wrist position, frequent breaks, task rotation, stretching exercises. OSHA ergonomic guidelines: identify risk factors (repetition, force, awkward postures, vibration, static positions), implement engineering controls (adjustable workstations, low-force tools), administrative controls (job rotation, rest breaks).

stem: A flow cytometry immunophenotyping panel on peripheral blood shows a population of lymphocytes positive for CD5, CD19, CD20dim, CD23, and negative for FMC7, CD10, and cyclin D1. The kappa:lambda ratio is 28:1. The diagnosis is:
options: Chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL) — the CD5+/CD19+/CD20dim/CD23+/FMC7- immunophenotype with kappa light chain restriction is characteristic of CLL
rationale: CLL immunophenotype: CD5+ (T-cell marker aberrantly expressed), CD19+, CD20dim, CD23+, FMC7-, light chain restriction (monoclonal — kappa:lambda ratio >3:1 or <0.3:1). Matutes/Moreau CLL scoring system: CD5+, CD23+, FMC7-, CD20dim, surface Ig dim = score 4-5/5 = CLL. Mantle cell lymphoma: CD5+, CD23-, FMC7+, CD20bright, cyclin D1+ (t(11;14)). Follicular lymphoma: CD10+, CD5-, bcl-2+. CLL diagnosis requires ≥5 × 10^9/L monoclonal B cells in blood per iwCLL criteria.

stem: A patient's hemoglobin A1c result by ion-exchange HPLC shows an unusual chromatogram with an unidentified peak in the S-window. The most likely cause is:
options: Hemoglobin variant (such as Hb S, Hb C, Hb D, or Hb E) interfering with the HbA1c measurement — the variant produces an abnormal chromatographic peak that may affect the accuracy of the reported HbA1c value
rationale: Ion-exchange HPLC for HbA1c: separates hemoglobin species by charge differences. Hemoglobin variants (S, C, D, E, and others) can co-elute with HbA1c peak or appear in unusual windows, causing: falsely elevated HbA1c (variant peak integrated with A1c), falsely decreased HbA1c (variant peak separated, reducing A1c area), or flagged results. When variant detected: report HbA1c with notation of variant interference, consider alternative method (immunoassay specific for N-terminal valine glycation, boronate affinity — less affected by variants), or use fructosamine/glycated albumin for monitoring.

stem: A MALDI-TOF mass spectrometry instrument identifies a Gram-negative rod from a blood culture bottle directly (without subculture) as Klebsiella pneumoniae with a score of 2.15. According to the manufacturer's interpretation criteria, a score ≥2.0 indicates:
options: High-confidence species-level identification — the protein fingerprint closely matches the reference spectrum in the database, enabling rapid ID from positive blood cultures
rationale: MALDI-TOF MS identification: measures ribosomal protein mass fingerprint → compares to reference database. Score interpretation (Bruker): ≥2.3 = highly probable species, 2.0-2.3 = secure genus/probable species, 1.7-2.0 = probable genus, <1.7 = unreliable. Score 2.15 = reliable species-level ID. Direct-from-blood-culture protocols (lysis-centrifugation, short incubation subculture) provide ID hours faster than traditional culture methods. Turnaround time: ~1 hour from positive blood culture to species ID. Revolutionized clinical microbiology workflow.

stem: A blood bank is implementing a computer crossmatch (electronic crossmatch) system. The prerequisites for electronic crossmatch include:
options: Two separate ABO/Rh typings on file (current and historical), negative antibody screen, validated computer system with logic to prevent release of ABO-incompatible blood, and no history of clinically significant antibodies
Only one blood type on file
rationale: Electronic (computer) crossmatch requirements per AABB Standards: (1) Two concordant ABO/Rh typings on record (one from current admission + one historical, or two separate current draws). (2) Negative antibody screen (current). (3) No record of clinically significant antibodies. (4) Validated computer system with logic to: verify ABO compatibility, prevent release of incompatible units, contain critical elements (patient ID, donor unit info, interpretation). Advantages: reduced TAT (no serological crossmatch needed), reduced labor, improved blood availability. If any prerequisite not met → serological crossmatch required.

stem: A laboratory is adopting ISO 15189:2022 for medical laboratory accreditation. The new edition emphasizes which additional requirement compared to the 2012 version?
options: Risk management integrated throughout the quality management system — ISO 15189:2022 requires systematic risk assessment and mitigation across all laboratory processes, from pre-analytical to post-analytical phases
rationale: ISO 15189:2022 key updates from 2012: (1) Risk-based thinking integrated throughout (risk assessment for all processes, not just as a standalone element). (2) Strengthened impartiality requirements. (3) Enhanced metrological traceability. (4) Expanded requirements for examination validation/verification. (5) Strengthened information management system requirements. (6) Greater emphasis on uncertainty of measurement. Risk management approach: identify risks → assess probability and impact → implement mitigation → monitor effectiveness → continuous review. Aligns with ISO 31000 risk management framework and harmonizes with ISO/IEC 17025.
